trajectory.description: Observation: First run crashed with std::bad_alloc immediately. Likely from torch.load loading the full 350k graph dataset into memory at once, or possibly a torch_geometric issue. Analysis: 350k molecules × tensors might consume a lot but should be feasible with 48GB RAM. The crash happened in 6.5s — too fast for actual training, likely during data loading. Let me first probe data loading carefully. Decision: First test loading the data and check memory; also reduce num_workers to avoid duplicating data in worker processes.

Observation: Data loads fine with probe.py (val took 18s). The first crash was likely from train.py with num_workers=4 — DataLoader workers fork the parent process duplicating memory, and 350k tensors via fork can cause issues. Analysis: I should reduce num_workers to 0 (or use persistent collation) for safety, and ensure data loads on main process. Decision: Set num_workers=0 to avoid fork bomb, then start a short training run (2 epochs) to validate pipeline + measure time.

trajectory.error_summary:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::bad_alloc'
  what():  std::bad_alloc
trajectory.description: Observation: Exit code 139 (segfault) before any output. Likely happening during torch.load or DataLoader spawn with workers. Probe.py worked, so torch.load itself works. Analysis: I ran train.py with num_workers=4. The issue: torch_geometric DataLoader with num_workers>0 forks the process and the large list of tensors causes memory issues. Also I should ensure the process actually starts. Decision: Set num_workers=0, run a small test first.

Observation: std::bad_alloc happens before any output, even before 'Loading data...'. Analysis: This is happening at import time, likely from torch_geometric or torch initialization. Let me check by running a simple import test first to isolate the issue. Could be memory issue with torch_scatter/sparse loading, or CPU thread allocation. Decision: Test imports first.

trajectory.error_summary:

trajectory.description: Observation: Even importing torch_geometric segfaults. python+torch ok but importing torch_geometric likely causes the bad_alloc. Let me verify by testing torch_geometric import isolation. Analysis: torch is 2.11.0+cu128 which is very new—possibly incompatible with installed torch-geometric/scatter binaries. Decision: Test imports separately to identify root cause.

trajectory.error_summary:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::bad_alloc'

trajectory.description: Observation: torch_scatter import causes std::bad_alloc with torch 2.11. The pre-installed torch_scatter binaries are likely incompatible with this torch version. Analysis: I need to avoid torch_scatter/torch_sparse and use pure PyTorch + torch_geometric ops. torch_geometric core itself might still work without scatter compiled extensions (it has fallback to torch.scatter_add). Let me check if torch_geometric works without torch_scatter. Decision: Run test_import without torch_scatter to see what's available; build model using pure pytorch scatter operations.

Observation: torch_scatter import causes std::bad_alloc with torch 2.11. Analysis: I should write a custom GNN that doesn't depend on torch_scatter/torch_sparse. torch_geometric core MessagePassing might also depend on these. Let me bypass torch_geometric entirely and write pure PyTorch with scatter_add_. Decision: First check if torch_geometric MessagePassing imports without scatter; if not, write pure-pytorch GNN.

trajectory.description: Observation: torch_scatter segfaults with torch 2.11. I need to bypass torch_geometric/torch_scatter entirely. Analysis: I'll write a pure PyTorch GNN using scatter_add_ (built-in to torch via index_add_). This avoids all compiled extensions. Decision: Write custom GIN with virtual node using pure torch ops, custom batching, and test it.

    preds = []

        for batch in loader:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            out = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.edge_attr, batch.batch)
